description: This figure presents phase amount (in mol) as a function of temperature (0 to 1300 °C) for two Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels, to demonstrate thermodynamic gradients of austenite stability. Subplot (a) corresponds to 9Al-10Mn-0.4C alloy, while subplot (b) corresponds to 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy. Three phases are tracked: BCC_A2 ferrite, FCC_A1 austenite, and KAPPA_E21 κ-carbide. For (a), BCC_A2 is the dominant phase at low temperatures with ~0.08 mol κ-carbide; above ~700 °C, BCC_A2 content drops sharply, FCC_A1 content rises to ~0.48 mol at 850 °C, and κ-carbide disappears rapidly. For (b), BCC_A2 dominates low temperatures with ~0.21 mol κ-carbide; above ~700 °C, BCC_A2 content drops, FCC_A1 content rises to ~1.0 mol at 950-1050 °C, and κ-carbide diminishes gradually. The figure confirms that carbon content alters phase equilibrium, and austenite decomposition is controlled by both chemical composition and tempering temperature, supporting that both alloy design and heat treatment regulate phase transformation behavior in lightweight steels.
